mainmatter.p: A deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\) is called positively univalent when for any positive \(A \in \mathcal{D}\), the type of positive objects \(B \in \mathcal{D}\) equipped with a thunkably invertible thunkable map \(f:B \vdash A\) is contractible.

frontmatter.title: The Univalence Condition for Deductive Systems

mainmatter.p: A deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\) is called positively univalent when for any positive \(A \in \mathcal{D}\), the type of positive objects \(B \in \mathcal{D}\) equipped with a thunkably invertible thunkable map \(f:B \vdash A\) is contractible.

A deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\) is called negatively univalent when for any negative \(A \in \mathcal{D}\), the type of negative objects \(B \in \mathcal{D}\) equipped with a linearly invertible linear map \(f:B \vdash A\) is contractible.
A deductive system is called univalent when it is both positively univalent and negatively univalent.

A deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\) is given by a type \(\mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\) of “objects”, and for every pair \(A,B \in \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\) a set \(\mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , B \right)}\)of morphisms, together with cut and identity laws:

ol.li: cut: for each \(A,B,C \in \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\) and \(f \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , B \right)}\) and \(g \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (B , C \right)}\), a morphism \(f;g \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , C \right)}\).

identity: for each \(A \in \mathbf{ob} _ {\mathcal{D}}\), a morphism \(1 _ {A} \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , A \right)}\) such that \(1 _ {A} ;f = f\) and \(f;1 _ {B}\) for each \(f \in \mathbf{hom} _ {\mathcal{D}} {\left (A , B \right)}\).

mainmatter.p: Let \(f:C \vdash D\) be a morphism in a deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\).

ol.li: We say that \(f\) is linear when for all \(g:B \vdash C\) and \(h:A \vdash B\), we have \(h; {\left (g;f \right)} = {\left (h;g \right)} ;f\).
We say that \(f\) is thunkable when for all \(g:D \vdash E\) and \(h : E \vdash F\), we have \(f; {\left (g;h \right)} = {\left (f;g \right)} ;h\).

mainmatter.p: Let \(f:A \vdash B\) be a morphism in a deductive system \(\mathcal{D}\). We say that \(f\) is (thunkably, linearly) invertible when there exists a (thunkable, linear) inverse to \(f\).

Let \(N \in \mathcal{D}\) be a negative object in a positively univalent deductive system; then the type of downshift structures on \(N\) is a proposition. In this case, we may refer to the property of \(N\) having a downshift.

details.p: Let \(w_0:N \vdash P_0\) and \(w_1:N \vdash P_1\) be two downshift structures on \(N\), whose inverse unwrapping maps are written \(u_0,u_1\) respectively. We wish to identity \({\left (P_0,w_0 \right)}\) with \({\left (P_1,w_1 \right)}\).

All four maps \(w_0,w_1,u_0,u_1\) are thunkable: the wrapping maps are thunkable by definition, and the unwrapping maps are thunkable because they are valued in a negative object. Thus we have a thunkably invertible thunkable map \(u_1;w_0 : P_1 \vdash P_0\); because \(\mathcal{D}\) is assumed positively univalent, we may identify the pairs \({\left (P_0, 1 _ {P_0} :P_0 \vdash P_0 \right)}\) and \({\left (P_1, {\left (u_1;w_0:P_1 \vdash P_0 \right)} \right)}\). Taking inverses and precomposing with \(w_0\) on both sides, we have \({\left (P_0,w_0 \right)} = {\left (P_1,w_1 \right)}\) using the fact that \(w_0\) is thunkable and inverse to \(u_0\).

mainmatter.p: A deductive system is called univalent when it is both positively univalent and negatively univalent.
